coredata.dc:title: Ultra-small carbon dots boost bioelectricity generation by accelerating extracellular electron transfer

coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xinqi
Yang, Chenhui
Wu, Jiaqi
Ning, Wei
Wang, Tianyi
Wang, Ruiwen
Liu, Shouxin
Li, Jian
Chen, Zhijun
Li, Shujun
coredata.dc:description: Bioelectrochemical systems incorporating carbon dots (CDs) show promise for both sustainable energy generation and wastewater treatment. The majority of currently available CDs are, however, potentially toxic and improperly sized for bioelectrochemical applications. In this study, ultra-small natural nitrogen-containing CDs were prepared using simple hydrothermal technology and common Hydrocotyle vulgaris leaves as starting material. These CDs, which have an average diameter of ∼1.3 nm, are readily taken up by bacterial cells and show excellent biocompatibility with the cells. When bound to the intracellular components of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1, the CDs substantially improve electron transport channels, leading to enhanced extracellular electron transfer (EET). This interaction not only stimulates the metabolism of S. oneidensis MR-1 but also increases the generation of bioelectricity. Biocells incorporating these CD-modified bacteria shows ∼11.2-, 2.0- and 13.1-fold increases in current, voltage and power output, respectively, compared with biocells incorporating unmodified bacteria. Our findings offer a viable method for producing biomass-based CDs that significantly improve EET, thereby amplifying bioenergy production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biomass
Ultra-small size
Extracellular electron transfer
Bioelectricity generation

Bacterial protein content test on carbon cloth
Investigation of effect of CDs on generation of bioelectricity
Morphology, structure and optical properties of CDs
Cellular internalization and cytocompatibility of CDs
Enhancement of bacterial adherence and metabolic rate by CDs
Enhanced performance in bioelectricity generation by CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support for this research was furnished by the Key Research and Development Plan of Heilongjiang Province (China) (Grant Nos. GZ20220115), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 32171716, 31890774), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2021M690572), the Heilongjiang Provincial Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. LBHZ21033), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 2572022BB05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing synthesis of CDs from Hydrocotyle vulgaris and CD-mediated promotion of bioelectricity production by S. oneidensis MR-1.

Structural characteristics and composition of CDs. (a) TEM micrographs and size distribution; (b) XRD pattern; (c) Raman spectrum; (d) XPS survey spectrum; (e) C 1s; (f) N 1s and (g) O 1s XPS spectra; (h) FT-IR spectrum; (i) Zeta potential.

Cellular internalization and cytocompatibility of CDs. TEM micrographs of S. oneidensis MR-1 cells (a) before and (c) after 6-h exposure to CDs; Cross-sectional TEM images of cells (b) before and (d) after incubation with CDs showing dark aggregates and indicating accumulation of CDs; (e) Growth kinetics of S. oneidensis MR-1 in LB medium in the presence of escalating concentrations (0–500 μg mL−1) of CDs; (f) NAG levels in CD-exposed and pristine S. oneidensis MR-1. (Statistical evaluation of data presented in (e) and (f) used n = 4 biological replicates; data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Significance thresholds were established using a two-tailed Student's t-test: *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001).) CLSM images of (g-j) pristine and (k-n) CD-treated S. oneidensis MR-1, labeled with calcein-AM and propidium iodide. The excitation/emission wavelengths for the dyes were 490/515 nm and 535/617 nm, respectively.

Augmented metabolic activity of MEC-CDs compared with MEC-UMs. (a) Biomass of anode biofilms; (b) Sodium lactate concentrations; (c) Riboflavin concentrations; (d) ATP levels; (e) ETSA values. (Statistical evaluation of data presented in (a-e) used n = 4 biological replicates; data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Significance thresholds were established using a two-tailed Student's t-test: *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001)); (f) CV profiles at a scan rate of 10 mvV s−1 for MEC-CDs and MEC-UMs. All MEC-CDs contained a uniform CD concentration of 50 μg mL−1.

Bioelectricity generation in bioelectrochemical systems. (a) Time profiles of current generation by MEC-CDs (50 μg mL−1) and MEC-UMs (statistical data from n = 3 biological replicates); (b) Voltage output profiles across four operational cycles, (c) polarisation and power curves CDs of supplemented microbial fuel cells (MFC-CDs, 50 μg mL−1); (d) polarisation and power curves of the unmodified MFC control group; (e), (f) The capability of MFC-CDs to power light-emitting diodes is exhibited; (g) A comparative analysis highlighting the fold increase in peak current and power output relative to prior studies; (h) A schematic representation of the CDs' role in enhancing both interbacterial and intrabacterial electrontransport networks within S. oneidensis MR-1.

head.ce:title: Ultra-small carbon dots boost bioelectricity generation by accelerating extracellular electron transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology, Northeast Forestry University, Ministry of Education, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology, Northeast Forestry University, Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beidahuang Group Heilongjiang Shanhe Farm Co., LTD, Nenjiang 161421, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beidahuang Group Heilongjiang Shanhe Farm Co., LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beidahuang Group Heilongjiang Shanhe Farm Co., LTD, Nenjiang 161421, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bioelectrochemical systems incorporating carbon dots (CDs) show promise for both sustainable energy generation and wastewater treatment. The majority of currently available CDs are, however, potentially toxic and improperly sized for bioelectrochemical applications. In this study, ultra-small natural nitrogen-containing CDs were prepared using simple hydrothermal technology and common Hydrocotyle vulgaris leaves as starting material. These CDs, which have an average diameter of ∼1.3 nm, are readily taken up by bacterial cells and show excellent biocompatibility with the cells. When bound to the intracellular components of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1, the CDs substantially improve electron transport channels, leading to enhanced extracellular electron transfer (EET). This interaction not only stimulates the metabolism of S. oneidensis MR-1 but also increases the generation of bioelectricity. Biocells incorporating these CD-modified bacteria shows ∼11.2-, 2.0- and 13.1-fold increases in current, voltage and power output, respectively, compared with biocells incorporating unmodified bacteria. Our findings offer a viable method for producing biomass-based CDs that significantly improve EET, thereby amplifying bioenergy production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Serious environmental pollution and energy shortages are driving the rapid development of sustainable, efficient and environmentally friendly technologies for clean energy production [1–4]. For example, bioelectrochemical systems have been used for wastewater treatment and degradation of hazardous substances, as well as in "green productivity devices" for the generation of bioenergy [5,6]. Shewanella spp., which are exemplars of electroactive bacteria, metabolize organic substrates to generate electrons, which can be subsequently transferred to electrodes or external acceptors via extracellular electron transfer (EET). This process has been used for the generation of bioenergy [7], e.g., bioelectricity [8], as well as for the biosynthesis of hydrogen [9], methane [10,11] and formic acid [12]. EET, whether it occurs directly, through C-type cytochromes or electron "shuttles", or indirectly, through an optimal redox mediator, is essential for harvesting bioenergy [13]. The efficiency of this process is, however, compromised by the non-conductive nature of bacterial cells, which limits real-world applications.

Modification of microorganisms with metal (Au, Ag, FeS2) nanoparticles [14,15], artificial conjugated oligoelectrolytes or conductive polymers (e.g., polypyrrole) [16] has been shown to enhance EET. Carbon dots (CDs), in particular, have shown promise for enhancing EET because of high electron transfer and photogenerated electron transduction [8,17–27]. Thereby, CDs improve significantly the microbial fuel cells (MFCs) performance in BESs, for example, S. oneidensis MR-1 modified by CDs achieve a 5.45 folds increasement of power density, reaching 491 mW m−2 [8], and under the light illumination, that power densiy of N, S doped CDs modified MFCs increase 2.6 folds and reaches up to 1697.9 mW m−2 [17]. Doping the CDs with heteroatoms (e.g., N atoms) further improves their photoelectrocatalytic performance because of additional active sites or because of the strong electronegativity of the heteroatoms [23]. However, most syntheses of N-doped CDs involve the use of N-containing compounds, which increases the cost and may have an adverse effect on biocompatibility [28]. An alternative approach would be to use very small CDs that have good dispersibility and biocompatibility, leading to greater cellular uptake and thus improved EET, but these are difficult to create [29].

In an attempt to overcome these problems, we have now synthesized ultra-small natural nitrogen-containing CDs using a simple hydrothermal process (Fig. 1 ). Hydrocotyle vulgaris, commonly known as marsh pennywort, was used as the sole source of carbon, thus avoiding the use of additional chemical agents. The resulting biomass-based CDs have an average diameter of ∼1.3 nm and show excellent biocompatibility, allowing them to be easily absorbed by bacterial cells. Owing to the graphitized structure and electron-rich nitrogen doping, CDs exhibit high electron transfer capability [30–32]. The interactions of the CDs with intracellular molecules form more effective pathways for electron movement and enhance electron transfer [33]. This enhancement of electron transfer, in turn, stimulates the metabolic activity of the cells and increases the production of electrons internally. The introduction of these tiny CDs led to a significant surge in bioenergy production, evidenced by an approximately 11.2-fold increase in the peak current of microbial electrochemical cells (MECs) and a 13.1-fold rise in the maximum power output of microbial fuel cells (MFCs). In this research, biomass with wide sources and low prices as raw materials are used to synthesize ultra-small CDs by a simple hydrothermal method. The electron transfer efficiency of S. oneidensis MR-1 is greatly improved by CDs, thus expanding the production of bioelectricity. Our research provides a cost-effective and efficient method for amplifying bioenergy production by using biomass-derived CDs to accelerate electron transfer processes.

Fresh leaves from H. vulgaris were used to synthesize the CDs. Luria-Bertani Broth and sodium lactate, used as the growth medium for S. oneidensis MR-1, were obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. Potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), which is needed for analysis of N-acetylglucosamine (NAG), was purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cellular protein levels were measured using a BCA assay kit obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. A combination of tris-HCl, sodium sulfite and triphenyltetrazolium chloride, all obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., was used to assess electron transport system activity (ETSA). The components of the phosphate buffer solution (PBS), KCl, NH4Cl, Na2HPO4·2H2O and Na2HPO4·12H2O, were all obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The anode material, carbon cloth (WOS 1002), was supplied by Taiwan Carbon Energy Technology.

The morphology, structure and composition of the CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Zeta potential measurements, UV absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. TEM was carried out using a Talos™ F200x G2 scanning transmission electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA); XRD spectra were recorded using an X'Pert Pro diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical B.V., Almelo, Netherlands); Raman spectra were recorded using a LabRAM HR Evolution confocal Raman microscope (Horiba, Ltd., Kyoto, Japan); XPS spectra were recorded using an ESCALAB 250Xi XPS microprobe (Thermo Fisher Scientific); FTIR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet iS10 instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific); Zeta potentials were measured using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Shanghai Sibaiqi Instrument System, Shanghai, China); UV absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1900 spectrophotometer (Beijing Puyang General Instrument, Beijing, China); and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using an LS-55 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific).
A mixture of fresh H. vulgaris (5 g) and deionized water (20 mL) was placed in a sealed 50 mL Teflon-coated autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h.

The biocompatibility of the CDs was evaluated by incubating S. oneidensis MR-1 with varying concentrations of CDs (0–500 μg mL−1) in Luria-Bertani medium at 30oC for 12 h, with a shaking speed of 160 rpm. The optical densities of the bacterial cultures at 600 nm were then measured using a microplate photometer.

An advanced Schales method [36] was used to quantitatively assess NAG levels in bacterial solutions in MECs using K3[Fe(CN)6] as an oxidizing agent. The evaluation involved a direct correlation between the concentration of NAG in the sample and the amount of [Fe(CN)6]3− reduced. K3[Fe(CN)6] was dissolved in sodium carbonate (0.5 mol L−1) aqueous solution to prepare K3[Fe(CN)6] working liquid (K3[Fe(CN)6] concentration of 0.5 g L−1). 1.5 ml bacterial solution in MECs was added to 2 ml working solution, heated in boiling water bath for 15 min, and absorbance at 420 nm was measured with supernatant solution. The concentration of K3[Fe(CN)6] in the sample is determined using a standard calibration curve that relates the absorbance at 420 nm to the concentration of K3[Fe(CN)6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cell viability by fluorescent staining
ce:section.ce:para: S. oneidensis MR-1 was incubated with CDs (100 μg mL−1) for 6 h. The cells were then pelleted by centrifugation at 2500 rpm for 5 min and resuspended in PBS (50 mmol L−1, 5 mL). Fluorescent stains were added to the bacterial PBS solutions. Calcein-AM (2 μmol L−1, 1 mL) was used to identify live cells and propidium iodide (4.5 μmol L−1, 1 mL) was used to identify dead cells. The bacterial cultures were then incubated in the dark at 37 °C for 15 min. Post-staining, the samples were analyzed using a TCS SP8 confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial protein content test on carbon cloth
ce:section.ce:para: S. oneidensis MR-1 with or without CDs modification was incubated by MECs for 6 h, the carbon cloth was removed and put into 2 mL centrifuge tube, and 1 mL 0.2 mol L−1 KOH solution was kept warm at 90 °C for 30 min, and then at 4 °C at 12000 rpm min−1. Centrifuge for 5 min, take 200 μL supernatant and set aside. 20 μL of the above sample solution and 200 μL of Bradord working solution were added to each hole of the 96-well plate, and were quickly mixed. After reaction at room temperature 20–30 °C for 5 min, the value of A595 was measured using an enzyme labeler. Calculate the protein concentration in solution according to the standard curve.

The ETSA in MECs incorporating CD-modified S. oneidensis MR-1 and unmodified S. oneidensis MR-1 (MEC-CDs and MEC-UMs, respectively) was determined. Following a 6-h incubation period, a mixture of 2,3,5-tribenzotetrazole ammonium chloride (0.4 %, 5 mL), sodium sulfite (0.36 %, 0.5 mL) and tris-HCl buffer (0.3 %, 1 mL) was added to the MEC, followed by incubation at 37oC for a further 30 min. The reaction was arrested by the addition of formaldehyde (5 mL), followed by incubation at 37oC for a further 10 min. Post-reaction, the sample was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. Acetone (4 mL) was added to the resultant pellet, which was then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The optical density at 485 nm of the resulting supernatant was recorded, and the protein concentration in the sample was quantified using a BCA assay kit. A standard curve was produced using the same method, but with Na2S2O4 solution instead of bacterial solution. The ETSA was calculated using Equation (1):
(Equation 1)
ETSA =
O D
485
× V
k w t
where OD 485 is the absorbance at 485 nm, V is the volume of acetone (mL), k is the slope of the standard curve, w is total protein weight (μg) and t is the duration of incubation within the MEC (h).

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) levels were determined using a commercial ATP assay kit. After incubation of MEC-CDs and MEC-UMs for 6 h at 30oC, the bacterial cultures were cooled to 4oC. An aliquot (2000 μL) of the culture was mixed with ATP detection lysis buffer (500 μL), followed by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 5 min at 4oC. A sample (100 μL) of the clear supernatant was combined with an equal volume of ATP detection working solution in a 96-well plate and luminescence was measured immediately. Concurrently, protein concentration in the sample was quantified using a BCA assay kit, using previously described methodology [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of effect of CDs on generation of bioelectricity
ce:section.ce:para: Generation of bioelectricity by S. oneidensis MR-1 in MEC-CDs and MEC-UMs at 30oC was evaluated using a tri-electrode monochamber MEC system, with a 42 mL capacity. The bioanode comprised a carbon cloth segment (1 cm × 2 cm), interfaced with a 0.5 mm titanium wire conductor, and a saturated Ag/AgCl system was used as the reference electrode. The electrolytic medium of both MEC-CDs and MEC-UMs was a mixture of PBS (50 mmol L−1) and sodium lactate solution (10 mmol L−1). Connection to a CHI1040C potentiostat (CH Instruments, Ltd.) facilitated real-time current monitoring, with data acquisition performed by a computing device. After depletion of sodium lactate, a cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve was constructed and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out, using CHI1040C and CHI 760E workstations, respectively.

Comparative bioelectricity assays with MEC-CDs and MEC-UMs were conducted at 30oC in bicameral MFCs, each chamber having a capacity of 120 mL. A CMI-7000S proton exchange membrane provided ionic separation between the cathodic and anodic compartments. Electrodes were fashioned from carbon cloth (2 cm × 4 cm) and electrically linked via 0.5 mm titanium wire to an external circuit set to a resistance of 1 kΩ. The catholyte contained K3[Fe(CN)6] (50 mmol L−1) and PBS solution (50 mmol L−1) at pH 7.0. The anolyte was a mixture of S. oneidensis MR-1 (3 mL, with or without incorporated CDs), PBS solution (50 mmol L−1) and sodium lactate solution (10 mmol L−1), also buffered to pH 7.0. A DAQ65410 data acquisition unit (Tektronix, Inc., Beaverton, OR, USA) was used to capture voltage-time profiles at 5-min intervals, from which power density calculations could be derived.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure and optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM analysis revealed that the CDs have a homogeneous particle size distribution, with an ultra-small mean diameter of ∼1.3 nm. High-resolution TEM imagery established a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2 a), corresponding to the [100] in-plane lattice parameter of graphitic structures [34]. The XRD spectra showed a pronounced broad peak at 2θ ∼24o (Fig. 2b), indicative of the interlayer distance of graphitic material, a feature commonly observed in the XRD profiles of numerous previously reported CDs [19,27]. The Raman spectrum showed two pronounced bands (Fig. 2c), a disorder-induced D band close to 1350 cm−1 and a graphitic G band close to 1580 cm−1, representing the disordered carbon configuration and the degree of crystal graphitization, respectively. The ratio of intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG) was ∼0.9, suggesting a prevalence of graphitization over disordered carbon within the CDs [31]. Collectively, the XRD and Raman spectra suggest that the CDs are crystalline, with a considerable degree of graphitization.

XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy were used to investigate the chemical composition and structural characteristics of the CDs. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 2d–S1) confirmed the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) entities, consistent with the innate composition of H. vulgaris. The atomic ratio of C, O and N in the CDs was determined to be 1.00 : 0.39: 0.04 (Table S1). The C 1s XPS profile (Fig. 2e) contained peaks at binding energies of 284.6, 286.0 and 288.3 eV, signifying the presence of C–C/CC, C–O/C–N and CO groups, respectively [35]. The N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2f), displayed peaks at binding energies of 397.6, 399.7 and 401.5 eV, which were ascribed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and quaternary N moieties, respectively [26]. The O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2g) showed peaks at binding energies of 531.5 and 532.8 eV, corresponding to carbonyl and carboxyl functionalities, respectively [27]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2h) had a broad absorption band at ∼3230 cm−1, which is characteristic of hydroxyl and amino groups, an absorption at 2928 cm−1, associated with bending modes of aromatic C–H bonds, and a sharp absorption at 1580 cm−1, indicative of carbonyl functionalities. Additional bands at 1400 and 1060 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of C–N and C–O–C linkages, respectively [24,25]. The FTIR spectrum of H. vulgaris showed characteristic peaks similar to CDs, indicating that the –NH/aromatic ring groups in CDs stem from the original H. vulgaris (Fig. S2). The XPS and FT-IR spectra confirm that the CDs predominantly consist of carbon and oxygen elements and also indicate the existence of various oxygenated surface moieties, such as phenolic and carboxylic groups, which are rich in electrons. This composition is further supported by the surface zeta potential measurement of approximately −18.5 mV (Fig. 2i), which points to a significant presence of electron-dense groups. That electron-rich nitrogen doping within CDs increases the electron density and active functional group content to augment the electron transfer capacity and stabilize the structure.

The optical properties of the CDs were investigated by UV–visible and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S3) shows an absorption band at ∼280 nm, which was attributed to π→π* electronic transitions, together with a second absorption band at 320 nm, which is associated with n→π* transitions of surface phenolic and carboxylic groups. The photoluminescence spectra of the CDs (Fig. S4) showed emissions ranging from blue to green, with fluorescence intensity varying with excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular internalization and cytocompatibility of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was used to examine morphological changes in the bacterial envelope caused by the presence of CDs. Pristine S. oneidensis MR-1 cells had a clear outline, with even surfaces (Fig. 3 a). In contrast, cells that had been incubated for 6 h with CDs showed distinct, sizeable, electron-dense regions on the bacterial envelope (Fig. 3c). Endocytosis of CDs by the bacteria was confirmed by TEM analysis of cross-sections. S. oneidensis MR-1 cells incubated with CDs had conspicuous electron-dense aggregates ranging in diameter from ∼12 nm to ∼41 nm (Fig. 3b and d and S5), presumably because of association of the CDs with intracellular constituents, such as cytochromes or other proteins [8]. It is notable that CDs are overwhelmingly attached to bacterial surfaces as depicted in Fig. 3c and rarely endocytosed by bacteria as depicted in Fig. 3d. That is likely due to the ultra-thin sections of bacterial samples (only 50–70 nm thick) and the inherent biological barriers to internalization within bacteria.

The cytocompatibility of the CDs was quantified by culturing S. oneidensis MR-1 cells in LB medium in the presence of escalating concentrations (0–500 μg mL−1) of CDs, followed by spectrophotometric absorbance readings at 600 nm to generate growth profiles (Fig. 3e). Following exposure to high concentrations (up to 500 μg mL−1) of CDs for >70 h, cellular viability remained above 95 %, signifying negligible inhibition of bacterial growth or reproduction by the CDs. To put this into context, operational concentrations of CDs in MECs and MFCs had a lower threshold of 50 μg mL−1. That bacterial growth rate with the ddition of CDs alone (50 μg mL−1) is almost similar to that without any medium as shown in Fig. S6, suggesting that CDs does not support as a leading nutrient substrate for bacterial growth. In addition, the does of CDs is much lower than that of sodium lactate (only 1/22) in the subsequent bioelectricity test. The release of NAG, a fundamental peptidoglycan monomer of bacterial cell walls, was assessed using an enhanced Schales procedure, with K3[Fe(CN)6] as the oxidant, and used to infer cellular integrity [36]. The consumption levels of [Fe(CN)6]3− in MECs incorporating bacteria exposed to CDs (50 μg mL−1) were marginally higher than in MECs with pristine bacteria (306.8 ± 1.7 μg mL−1 vs. 289.3 ± 1.9 μg mL−1), suggesting minimal cellular impairment by the CDs (Fig. 3f).

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to compare the viability of CD-treated S. oneidensis MR-1 with that of pristine S. oneidensis MR-1. Calcein-AM, a hydrophobic compound that can diffuse through cell membranes, is hydrolyzed by esterases within living cells to produce fluorescent calcein. Propidium iodide, in contrast, is a nucleic acid intercalator that is excluded from viable cells and penetrates only cells with compromised membranes, when a red fluorescence is emitted upon binding to DNA. CLSM assessments revealed no discernible variation in red or green fluorescence between MECs with pristine bacteria and those with CD-treated bacteria (50 μg mL−1) (Fig. 3g–n). The closely matched green-to-red fluorescence ratios (84.21 % vs. 80.42 %) are indicative of a comparable live/dead cell distribution. Collectively, these findings show that the CDs have minimal cytotoxicity and are very compatible with bacterial cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of bacterial adherence and metabolic rate by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Enhanced formation of bacterial biofilm and increased metabolic rate are crucial for bioelectrogenesis. Surface engineering strategies, such as augmenting the topographical complexity of the electrodes, have been shown to facilitate microbial adhesion—an approach substantiated by Yuan and colleagues [37]. The CDs in our electrogenic system increase the surface negative charge on the bacteria via functional moieties, including phenolic and carboxylic groups, thereby augmenting the affinity of the microbes for the carbon cloth anode. Quantitative analysis revealed a 27 % upsurge in biofilm density for S. oneidensis MR-1 incorporating CDs compared with the non-engineered counterpart (7.3 ± 0.3 μg cm−2 vs. 5.7 ± 0.3 μg cm−2) (Fig. 4 a). These data point to a significant enhancement in microbial attachment and proliferation, which can be attributed to incorporation of CDs.

The metabolic vigor of S. oneidensis MR-1 that incorporates CDs was inferred from various metabolic indices, including substrate degradation rates, content of secretions, ATP synthesis, intracellular electron flux and electrochemical performance. The speed of lactate consumption, an indicator of metabolic intensity, was elevated in MEC-CDs, compared with MEC-UMs, as evidenced by a lower residual lactate concentration measured via high-performance liquid chromatography (0.29 ± 0.10 μg mL−1 vs. 0.47 ± 0.03 μg mL−1) (Fig. 4b). Riboflavin, a redox-active metabolite synthesized by S. oneidensis MR-1, was present in greater amounts in MEC-CDs than in MEC-UMs (0.074 ± 0.0083 μg mL−1 vs. 0.047 ± 0.0029 μg mL−1) (Fig. 4c), confirming the role of the CDs in enhancing substrate catabolism and release of redox effectors.

ATP concentration, a proxy for energy metabolism [38], was markedly higher in CD-modified bacterial cells (1.20 ± 0.16 nmol mg−1 protein−1) than in unmodified cells (0.64 ± 0.06 nmol mg−1 protein−1) (Fig. 4d), underscoring the ability of the CDs to amplify energy reserves and thereby potentiate generation of bioelectricity, as suggested by Yang et al. [8]. The ETSA, which is estimated by the capacity of internal bacterial electrons to reduce 2,3,5-triphenyltetrazole ammonium chloride [39], showed a substantial increase in intracellular electron generation in CD-modified cells (0.52 ± 0.08 μg mg−1 h−1) compared with unmodified cells (0.11 ± 0.03 μg mg−1 h−1) (Fig. 4e), confirming the role of the CDs in internal charge production and ruling out any assay interference.

EET mechanisms include both direct transfer, via cell membrane-associated cytochromes and conductive pili, and indirect pathways, mediated by soluble redox factors, such as flavins [40,41]. CV analyses of electrochemical activity in MEC-CDs vs. MEC-UMs established that the redox signals are of bacterial origin, since such signals were absent in the CV profiles of CDs and the anode carbon cloth in the absence of bacteria (Fig. S7). Electrochemical analysis delineated the redox behavior of both MECs and MEC-CDs within a potential window of −0.60 V to 0.40 V (Fig. 4f). MEC-UMs exhibited a redox couple with cathodic and anodic peak currents of −61.8 μA at −0.2 V and 72.5 μA at −0.17 V, respectively, which were attributed to the intrinsic transmembrane electron transfer mechanisms facilitated by S. oneidensis MR-1 cytochromes [22]. In contrast, MEC-CDs showed enhanced electrochemical activity, evidenced by two pronounced redox couples with augmented peak currents. The initial redox couple displayed a cathodic peak of −208.4 μA at −0.48 V and an anodic peak of 195.5 μA at 0.02 V, which were attributed to facilitation of riboflavin excretion and subsequent promotion of indirect extracellular electron transfer [42]. The subsequent redox couple with a cathodic peak of −222.8 μA at −0.29 V and an anodic peak of 287.8 μA at −0.13 V can be linked to direct bacterial transmembrane electron transfer processes. The substantial escalation in peak redox currents in the MEC-CDs confirms the role of the CDs in potentiating direct electron transfer by forming complexes with the cytochromes of S. oneidensis MR-1 [43,44]. EIS showed diminished electrical resistance in MEC-CDs relative to MEC-UMs (Fig. S8), confirming the hypothesis that CDs mitigate internal resistance and bolster electron flux within MEC configurations.

Taken together, the increases in bacterial adhesion, propagation on the anodic surface, substrate utilization, extracellular secretion, intracellular ATP concentration, generation of intracellular electrons and electrochemical performance all confirm that CDs increase the metabolism of S. oneidensis MR-1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced performance in bioelectricity generation by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The augmentation of electrogenic capabilities in S. oneidensis MR-1 through incorporation of CDs was evaluated by establishing MECs and MFCs in the presence and absence of CDs.

The MEC-CDs achieved a 11.2-fold increase in peak current generation compared with the MEC-UMs (187.2 ± 66.5 μA at 71 h vs. 15.4 ± 7.24 μA at 123 h) (Fig. 5 a). In the absence of exogenous substrate, the MEC-CDs produced a maximum current of 0.8 μA, which exceeded that produced by the MEC-UMs, although both were markedly inferior to that of substrate-augmented MECs (Fig. S9). This suggests that CDs do not enhance bioelectricity generation by acting as microbial sustenance. Comparing the current output profiles of the MEC-CDs and the MEC-UMs showed an accelerated rise and fall in current generation by the MEC-CDs, indicating that CDs enhance the metabolic velocity of S. oneidensis MR-1 and lead to swifter depletion of the substrate lactate. The cumulative charge transferred in the MEC-CDs was also markedly greater than that transferred in the MEC-UMs (Fig. S10).

A bipartite chamber system, each chamber with 120 mL capacity and a fixed resistance of 1.0 kΩ, was used to assemble both CD-integrated and standard MFCs (termed MFC-CDs and MFC-UMs, respectively). The peak voltage output of the MFC-CDs was substantially higher than that of the MFC-UMs across four operational cycles, with a 2.0-fold increase in peak voltage (Fig. 5b). Power density andpolarization curves of both MFC-CDs and MFC-UMs were investigated on varying the external resistance (100 Ω-100 wΩ) of the system (Fig. 5c and d). The optimal power density of the MFC-CDs was 13.1-fold higher than that of the MFC-UMs (331.7 mW m−2 vs. 23.6 mW m−2).

To demonstrate a practical application of the MFC-CDs, an assembly of three "tree" configurations of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) was powered by six MFC-CDs (Fig. 5e and f). After activation, the luminosity of the LED lamps was consistently maintained, demonstrating the potential of the MFC-CDs for robust power generation. Compared with previous studies on bacterial bioelectricity augmentation [8], CD-modified S. oneidensis MR-1 showcases a marked enhancement in efficiency (Fig. 5g). Because of the exceptional electron mediation properties of the CDs, well-organized pathways for rapid inter- and intra-bacterial electron transfer were established, bolstering the efficiency of EET processes. The accelerated electron transfer dynamics enhance metabolic activities and consequently amplify electron flux (Fig. 5h).

In this study, we have engineered diminutive nitrogen-doped CDs employing the cost-effective Helianthus vulgaris foliage as a carbonaceous precursor. CDs augment the adhesion of bacteria on carbon cloth anode because CDs` functional moieties (such as phenolic and carboxylic groups) increase the surface negative charge on the bacteria. High electron transfer originates from the graphitized structure and electron-rich nitrogen doping. Owing to their diminutive dimensions and favorable biocompatibility profiles, these carbon dots were readily internalized by bacterial cells via phagocytosis and subsequently interacted with intracellular macromolecules, inclusive of proteins, peptides, and enzymes [19]. The distinguished electron-conducting attributes of the carbon dots facilitate the formation of an efficient intracellular electron transport network, thereby amplifying the extracellular electron transfer (EET) efficiency. This acceleration in electron mobility augmented the cellular metabolic processes, leading to a substantial rise in endogenous electron flux. The bioelectrochemical systems modified with these CDs exhibited an enhancement of approximately 11.2-fold, 2.0-fold, and 13.1-fold in maximal current, voltage, and power density, respectively.

Relative to antecedent research, the current investigation delineates several advancements: (1) Utilization of a naturally derived, economical biomass for CDs synthesis. (2) An eco-benign and facile synthetic route for CDs that obviates the need for exogenous nitrogen-doping agents due to the inherent nitrogenous content of the raw material. (3) Fabrication of CDs possessing nanoscopic size, aqueous solubility, and biocompatibility, which promotes bacterial uptake and intracellular electron channel construction. (4) A substantial enhancement in bioelectricity output, with significant increases in both maximal current and power output.

Our designed 'bio-abiotic' interface system significantly bolsters microbial bioelectricity production. Exploitation of low-cost biomass for high-value applications heralds a pivotal advancement for EET-related fields, encompassing the augmentation of plant photosynthetic efficiency, microbial biohydrogen generation, and methane production.

Financial support for this research was furnished by the Key Research and Development Plan of Heilongjiang Province (China) (Grant Nos. GZ20220115), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 32171716, 31890774), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2021M690572), the Heilongjiang Provincial Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. LBHZ21033), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 2572022BB05).

Xinqi Guo: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chenhui Yang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jiaqi Wu: Investigation. Wei Ning: Resources. Tianyi Wang: Resources. Ruiwen Wang: Investigation. Shouxin Liu: Investigation. Jian Li: Investigation. Zhijun Chen: Resources, Funding acquisition. Shujun Li: Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
